items.q: What property of a sound wave determines its pitch?
items.correct: The frequency — higher frequency means higher pitch.
items.misconception: Amplitude determines pitch — a bigger wave is a higher-pitched note.
items.random_wrong: The speed of the wave determines pitch — faster waves sound higher.
items.why: Students conflate the two main wave properties: amplitude relates to volume, frequency to pitch, but they often swap them.

items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.

items.q: What does the spacing of field lines on a diagram indicate?
items.correct: The strength of the field — closer lines mean a stronger field.
items.misconception: Field lines are equally important regardless of spacing — only the arrows matter.
items.random_wrong: Wider spacing means a stronger field because the lines have more room to act.
items.why: Students treat field lines as a count rather than a density-based representation; they miss that the convention encodes strength via spacing.

items.q: For y = a × b, uncertainty rule is:
items.why: Correct answer: Add percentages. “Add absolutes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If r has 2% uncertainty, r² has uncertainty:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 4%. “1%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A length is (10.0 ± 0.1) cm. Percentage uncertainty:
items.why: The real figure is 1.0. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
